intake.finding: Problem well-validated: SharedWorker (a final HTML Living Standard) was the last major mobile browser gap, disabled on Chrome Android since 2012 over process-lifecycle concerns. Sustained developer demand is documented across a 13-year tracking bug, a community Interop 2026 request, and third-party library friction. Firefox and Safari already ship on mobile, making Chrome the last holdout. Enabling an existing standardized API is clearly the right intervention layer.
intake.limitations: Demand is largely self-reported developer comments and library issues, not a structured survey; no attributable partner production-deployment evidence is named publicly (the OT extension references an unnamed major partner).

incubation.finding: Not applicable as an API incubation: this is a Chromium catches-up enablement of an already-final, multi-engine standard (HTML SharedWorker). No new surface was incubated for this feature. The one design question this work exposed (unpredictable agent-cluster killing under memory pressure) was incubated separately as the extendedLifetime option (whatwg/html#11205; distinct feature 5138641357373440) rather than folded into this feature's API.
incubation.limitations: Marked not-relevant for THIS feature's API; the lifecycle/spec question it exposed remains open in whatwg/html#11205 and is tracked under a different feature, so it is not tested here either.

prototype.finding: The SharedWorker code path already shipped on desktop, so the Android work was enablement plus a runtime flag and an OT test rather than a greenfield prototype. Implementation CLs (runtime_enabled_features, SharedWorkerOnAndroid OT test, UseCounter, UMA) landed Jul-Aug 2025. No documented task-based prototype, friction log, or varied-cohort prototype phase precedes the dev trial; the Android-specific lifecycle risk was explicitly deferred to the origin trial for validation rather than prototyped away first.
prototype.limitations: No public prototype/friction-log artifact; reliance on desktop parity as a proxy for Android correctness is a known gap (Chromium WPT runs on Linux, not Android).

developerTrials.finding: A developer trial behind a flag shipped at M140 alongside the origin trial. Devtrial instructions point only to generic MDN SharedWorker docs (not Android-specific guidance). No documented cohort recruitment, representativeness report, or friction findings; the substantive developer feedback was collected via the OT (owner solicited feedback Oct 2025 and Jan 2026) rather than a structured dev-trial cohort.
developerTrials.limitations: No public cohort/methodology/limitations artifact; participation and task completion not separately reported from OT registrations.

wideReview.finding: Other-engine positions are strong and verified (Firefox and Safari Shipped/Shipping on mobile; Safari since iOS/Safari 16). The API is a final published standard, so TAG review is reasonably Not applicable. However, the cached record shows security_review_status and privacy_review_status both Pending and never visibly resolved in the public record, and no attributable WebKit/Gecko mobile-lifecycle perspective was captured into this feature's review despite whatwg/html#11205 explicitly asking for one.
wideReview.limitations: Pending security/privacy review status is a genuine gap (not no-signal-treated-as-support); lifecycle behavioral parity between Chrome Android and WebKit/Gecko mobile is unspecified and unverified.

experiment.finding: Origin Trial M140-144, extended to M147, was well-scoped: explicit hypotheses (termination frequency, API sufficiency, need for a spec countermeasure), dedicated metrics (Content.SharedWorker.Host.DestructionSource UMA; kSharedWorkerStartOnAndroid use counter), third-party OT support, and an extension tied to a concrete partner need. But the evidence base is thin: 34 total sign-ups; at extension time the team stated it did not yet have the statistical volume to draw firm conclusions; the feared Worker-connection-lost events were confirmed real but rare; and the ship decision rested on crash-related terminations being not significant on Android vs desktop and no reports of unstable SharedWorkers rather than a pre-registered threshold.
experiment.limitations: No published OT readout with denominators, statistical method, or thresholds; 'no reports' is weak evidence; the unnamed major partner's results are not public.

prepareToShip.finding: Intent to Ship (2026-03-26) followed process and received LGTM1 (contingent on OT findings, which were supplied), LGTM2, and LGTM3. The WebView risk was surfaced and resolved in-review (single renderer process; shared worker runs with its creator). Rollout is a Finch flag (SharedWorker) enabling rollback, and post-ship metric retention was committed. Gaps: documentation is MDN-only with no Chrome-authored article/sample for this feature or its Android lifecycle caveat, and security/privacy review remained Pending.
prepareToShip.limitations: No dedicated Chrome-authored docs/samples; formal review gate states not confirmed resolved in the public record.

release.finding: Shipped enabled by default in M148 (stable desktop promoted 2026-05; Android/WebView at M148). Coordinated across the Chrome 148 beta blog, release notes, and New in Chrome 148, with modest independent press coverage (Notebookcheck). Caveat: the public framing reused the pre-OT hypothesis (a recent discussion suggests the lifecycle issue might not be as significant) rather than reporting the measured OT findings, so the evidentiary basis is understated in user-facing comms.
release.limitations: No dedicated deep-dive article or sample; messaging does not surface the measured low-but-real termination rate or the open lifecycle spec question.

adoption.finding: Strong pre-ship demand (13-year bug thread, Interop 2026 request, LiveStore single-tab fallback workaround, Pusher/RxDB/wa-sqlite ecosystem references) and 34 OT sign-ups indicate real interest. First-party Chrome usage telemetry is now cached for the exact feature counter (kSharedWorkerStartOnAndroid, ChromeStatus bucket 5626; scope exact, 162 daily points). During the OT/dev-trial the counter was effectively silent — 0% at stable (2025-09-02) and at day 30, ~0.000001% at day 90 (2025-12-15), and ~0.0000004% at day 180 (2026-03-01), consistent with opt-in-only exposure and 34 sign-ups. Default enablement produced a clear step-change: ~0.005904% of measured Chrome page loads at M148 stable (2026-05-05), ~0.007707% at day 30 (2026-06-04), a peak of ~0.012877% (2026-07-11), and ~0.007972% at the latest observation (2026-07-21); M148 day 90/day 180 are not yet available (feature ~78 days past stable at observation). These values are the share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads on which the counter fired at least once — not unique developers, sites, or users — and no adoption target was predeclared, so the low-but-present level is described, not scored. Production-deployment, unique-site, and retention evidence remain absent.
adoption.limitations: Use counter is a page-load share (counter fired at least once), not unique sites/developers/users, production deployment, task success, or end-user benefit; counter instrumentation may include probing, and UMA reflects participating Chrome clients with enterprise/opt-out populations underrepresented. No predeclared adoption target, so low usage is not scored as failure. The metrics cache also surfaced two CSS-property counters ('content' bucket 74 at ~0.8%, 'source' bucket 667 at 0%) that text-matched the UMA name 'Content.SharedWorker.Host.DestructionSource' and are excluded as unrelated to SharedWorker. The metrics layer was collected 2026-07-22 and postdates the original 2026-07-19 evidence base; process-phase assessments are unchanged.

support.finding: OT feedback channels were active (owner solicited feedback twice during the trial). A Trial Usage alert fired and was determined to be a false alarm, indicating monitoring exists. Post-ship support volume, regressions, and issue resolution are not yet observable (feature ~2.5 months past stable as of 2026-07-22).
support.limitations: No post-ship support/regression/issue-resolution data; the committed kSharedWorkerStartOnAndroid use counter is now reporting page-load share (see usage-timeline-5626-cached), but support-specific signals (regression volume, issue counts, resolution times) and the Content.SharedWorker.Host.DestructionSource UMA distribution are not in the cached telemetry.

deprecation.finding: Not applicable: this is an enablement of an existing standard, not a restriction, replacement, or removal. No reverse trial, migration, or removal path is involved.
deprecation.limitations: Marked not-relevant because no deprecation/removal activity exists; the OT-expiry fallback (SharedWorker disabled again on Android after the trial) was a stated trial risk that is now superseded by default enablement.

developerValue.finding: Addresses a real, durable, repeatedly-articulated developer need (share a single WebSocket/SSE connection and persistent state such as WASM SQLite across mobile tabs) and closes the last major-browser gap. Demand is evidenced over 13 years and across frameworks/libraries (RxDB, LiveStore, wa-sqlite, Pusher).
developerValue.limitations: Realized value depends on adoption; the use counter now shows low-but-present usage but no unique-site/production evidence.

endUserImpact.finding: Indirect end-user benefit expected (fewer duplicated background connections -> less battery/data) but never measured directly. The principal end-user-relevant risk (a shared worker silently killed under memory pressure, breaking app state) was monitored during the OT and found low relative to desktop, but confirmed real and not fully characterized; the underlying behavior is unspecified.
endUserImpact.limitations: No end-user study; resource/battery benefit is inferred, not measured.

adoption.finding: Pre-ship demand is strong and multi-source (13-year bug, Interop 2026 request, LiveStore fallback, RxDB/Pusher/wa-sqlite references). Post-ship, the exact feature use counter (bucket 5626) now shows low-but-present, roughly stable usage with daily variance: ~0.005904% of measured Chrome page loads at M148 stable (2026-05-05), ~0.007707% at day 30 (2026-06-04), a peak of ~0.012877% (2026-07-11), and ~0.007972% at the latest observation (2026-07-21) — roughly four orders of magnitude above the near-zero OT period (0% at stable/day30; ~0.000001% at OT day 90). This is the share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads where the counter fired at least once, not unique developers/sites/users or production deployment. No adoption target was predeclared for this feature and there is no corroborating production/retention/unique-site evidence, so durable adoption remains unscored; the measured level and trend are described above.
adoption.limitations: Page-load share (counter fired at least once), not unique sites/developers/users, production deployment, task success, or end-user benefit. No predeclared adoption target, so low usage is not scored as failure and a higher value would not alone be success; M148 day 90/day 180 checkpoints not yet available. No production/retention/unique-site evidence.

interoperability.finding: This is the defining win: after M148, SharedWorker is available in all major browsers on mobile, resolving the Interop 2026 rationale (implemented in every browser except Chrome on Android, so it cannot be used as a core feature). Firefox and Safari ship on mobile; the API is a final standard. Caveats: Chromium WPT runs on Linux not Android, and behavioral/lifecycle parity across mobile engines is unspecified (whatwg/html#11205 open).
interoperability.limitations: Android-side conformance not independently verified via WPT; lifecycle parity unspecified.

implementationQuality.finding: Implementation reuses the shipped desktop code path behind a runtime flag; OT found crash-related terminations not significant on Android vs desktop; dedicated UMA and use-counter monitoring added and committed post-ship; WebView process model clarified in review. But the feared termination events are real (observed, rare), the lifecycle behavior is implementation-defined (spec gap), and the team shipped before it had statistical volume to draw firm conclusions.
implementationQuality.limitations: No statistical characterization published; Android WPT coverage absent.

evidenceAndCommunication.finding: Process rigor is high (stated hypotheses, metrics, an OT findings summary, three LGTMs with one contingent on findings, committed monitoring). But the ship decision rested on not-significant/no-reports rather than a pre-registered threshold while the team admitted insufficient statistical volume; public messaging reused the pre-OT hypothesis instead of the measured findings; docs are MDN-only; and security/privacy review stayed Pending in the public record.
evidenceAndCommunication.limitations: Security/privacy review resolution not visible; no published OT statistical readout.

support.finding: Too early to assess: feedback channels were active during the OT and monitoring exists, but post-ship support volume, regressions, and resolution times are not yet observable.
support.limitations: No post-ship support data at cutoff.

overall.finding: A well-motivated, methodically-trialed catches-up enablement that closes a 13-year interoperability gap with strong developer demand. Mixed because the ship rests on modest statistical evidence, the core lifecycle risk is confirmed-real-but-rare and remains under-specified, documentation is thin, and release comms understated the measured basis. Early post-ship usage is low-but-present and roughly stable (~0.006-0.013% of measured Chrome page loads over the first ~11 weeks, with no predeclared adoption target), which does not by itself revise the mixed call. Net direction is positive; the weak spots are evidence/docs/communication, not the problem or the API.
overall.limitations: Adoption now partially measured (low page-load share, no target, no production/unique-site evidence); post-ship support outcomes still unknown.

supports: SharedWorker is a final published standard; defines SharedWorker/SharedWorkerGlobalScope; does not fully specify process-lifecycle/OOM-kill behavior
sources.limitations: Living standard, no fixed publication date; lifecycle/OOM behavior under-specified (per whatwg/html#11205).

supports: Chrome 148 promoted to the stable channel (desktop) in May 2026, anchoring the M148 enablement event

supports: Use-counter bucket 5626 (kSharedWorkerStartOnAndroid) is the defined adoption metric for this feature

sources.publisher: Chrome Platform Status feature-popularity timeline (cached daily JSON; bucket 5626 = kSharedWorkerStartOnAndroid)
supports: Exact feature use counter for this feature (scope exact, 162 daily points). Measured share of Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads on which the counter fired at least once. Near-zero during OT/dev-trial: atStable and day30 0%; day90 (2025-12-15) 0.000001%; day180 (2026-03-01) 0.0000004%; first positive 2025-11-08 at 0.00000001%. Step-change at default enablement: M148 atStable (2026-05-05) 0.005904%; day30 (2026-06-04) 0.00770696%; peak 0.01287707% (2026-07-11); latest 0.00797191% (2026-07-21); latest snapshot 0.0082061% (2026-07-20). M148 day90/day180 not yet available.
sources.limitations: Share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads where the counter fired at least once — not unique developers, sites, users, successful tasks, satisfaction, production deployment, or end-user benefit. Counter instrumentation may include detection/probing; UMA reflects participating Chrome clients (enterprise/opt-out populations underrepresented). The metrics cache also contained two CSS-property counters ('content' bucket 74 ~0.8%, 'source' bucket 667 0%) that text-matched the UMA name 'Content.SharedWorker.Host.DestructionSource' and are excluded as unrelated to SharedWorker. No predeclared adoption target; low values are not automatically failure.

successes: Closed a 13-year gap; after M148 SharedWorker is available in all major browsers on mobile, validating the Interop 2026 rationale.

Honest experiment design: explicit hypotheses (termination frequency, API sufficiency, spec-countermeasure need), dedicated metrics (Content.SharedWorker.Host.DestructionSource UMA; kSharedWorkerStartOnAndroid use counter), third-party OT support, and committed post-ship monitoring.
The OT extension was tied to a concrete (unnamed) partner need and was scrutinized rather than rubber-stamped, and the WebView process-model risk was surfaced and resolved during the ship review.
Adjacent spec work (extendedLifetime via whatwg/html#11205) advanced in parallel as a real countermeasure to the lifecycle concern, rather than ignoring it.
failures: Public messaging (Chrome 148 beta blog) reused the pre-OT hypothesis framing instead of reporting the measured OT findings, understating the evidentiary basis for the ship decision.
Documentation is effectively MDN-only; there is no Chrome-authored article or sample covering the Android process-lifecycle caveat or graceful-termination guidance for this feature.
security_review_status and privacy_review_status were both Pending in the public record and never visibly resolved before default enablement.

The feared lifecycle failure (Worker connection lost) was confirmed real, yet at extension time the team stated it lacked statistical volume to draw firm conclusions; the ship proceeded on not-significant/no-reports rather than a pre-registered bar.
counterfactuals: If a termination-rate threshold and a representative-cohort exposure target had been pre-registered before the OT, the ship decision would rest on a clearer evidentiary bar and the insufficient-statistical-volume gap would have been confronted before sign-off rather than after.

If the beta blog and release notes had stated the measured finding (crash-related terminations not significant vs desktop) alongside the known caveat (lifecycle still under-specified in the HTML Standard), developers would calibrate expectations instead of inferring desktop parity from re-enabled.

If a dedicated Chrome-authored doc/sample had covered the Android lifecycle caveat and the extendedLifetime fallback, third parties such as LiveStore would be less likely to ship single-tab fallbacks on Chrome Android.
If WPT ran on Android for Chromium, implementation parity and the lifecycle edge cases could be tested rather than asserted from Linux runs.
skillImprovements: Phase 05 (experiment) rule: an OT findings summary at ship time must restate the original hypotheses, the pre-registered thresholds, denominators, and explicitly flag when statistical volume is insufficient; an Intent to Ship should not be approvable on no-reports alone when the team has stated it lacks statistical volume.

Phase 07 (release) rule: release communications for experiment-backed shipments must cite the measured findings (not the pre-experiment hypothesis) and state known caveats; a catches-up framing must not imply parity where platform behavior differs.

Phase 06/07 rule: catches-up enablements still require (a) a dedicated doc/sample covering platform-specific caveats (here: Android lifecycle) and (b) visible resolution of security/privacy review status before Enabled by default.

Add a retrospective regression-eval case for low-risk catches-up features where shipment on modest evidence is defensible but the real failure modes are evidence, docs, and communication rather than the API or the problem.

missingEvidence: Unique-site, production-deployment, and retention evidence for SharedWorker on Android (the exact feature use counter kSharedWorkerStartOnAndroid is now cached at ~0.006-0.013% of measured page loads through 2026-07-21; M148 day 90/day 180 checkpoints are pending).
Published OT statistical readout with denominators, thresholds, and the unnamed major partner's results.
Resolution of security_review_status and privacy_review_status (both Pending in the cached record).
Post-ship support/regression volume and resolution times (the use counter reports usage but not support burden).
WebKit/Gecko mobile-lifecycle perspectives captured into this feature's review (whatwg/html#11205 remains open).
